rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 26, on Line 1, CROSS DEFENDANT COLE HARDWARE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Sunset Scavengers And Lylian Sifuentes Cross-Complaints The motion is transferred to department 501 to be heard on January 14, 2020 at 9:30 a.m. That department handles all matters involving injury to real property per SF Local Rule 8.10A1. Parties are to comply with Local Rule 2.7B and provide courtesy copies of opposition and reply no later than January 2, 2020 with an updated cover letter reflecting the new hearing date. The related motions set in department 302 on January 8, 2020 and January 14, 2020 are also transferred to department 501 to be heard on January 14, 2020 at 9:30 a.m. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRENT MARSH VS. MICHAEL CAMPESINO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Michael Campesino Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 26, on Line 4, Defendant Michael Campesino Demurrer To Complaint Defendant's demurrer to the complaint is overruled and Defendant's answer is due within 5 calendar days. A "complaint is invulnerable to general demurrer if on any theory it states a cause of action." (Johnson v. Clark (1936) 7 Cal.2d 529, 536.) Among other grounds, a tenant is guilty of unlawful detainer if under Code of Civ. Proc. § 1161(3) he continues in possession after failing to perform conditions or covenants of the lease and failing to cure a curable breach or if under § 1161(4) he commits waste, nuisance, or uses the premises for an unlawful purpose. Plaintiff's complaint sufficiently states a cause of action under § 1161(4) on the basis of nuisance. "California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1161(4) further provides that a tenancy may be terminated upon three days' notice for the tenant . . . Items 2, 5, 6, 7, 11, 12 already occurred and were acts of nuisance . . . acts of nuisance determined to be serious, recurring or continuing are not curable by law." (Compl., Ex. 2.) In light of these allegations, regardless of the curability of the alleged lease violations, the complaint survives demurrer. Defendant's argument that because a § 1161(4) notice "was served within a curable notice" the notice is defective is rejected. (Def.'s Reply at 3.) Defendant does not provide any support for this suggestion, nor is the court aware of any. In fact, case law supports the opposite conclusion. (See Hinman v. Wagnon (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 24, 29 [suggesting that a notice would survive demurrer if there were facts alleged that bring it under § 1161(4)]; Richard v. Degen & Brody (1960) 181 Cal.App.2d 289, 294 ["Informing a tenant that he must cure within three days a breach which he knows cannot be cured and that his failure so to do will bring on an unlawful detainer action, has no tendency to confuse or mislead him."].) Contrary to Defendant's argument (Reply at 4), Richard was later disapproved on different grounds having nothing to do with the issue in this case. (See Kendall v. Ernest Pestana, Inc. (1985) 40 Cal.3d 488, 496-498 [holding that where commercial lease provides for assignment or sublease only with prior consent of lessor, such consent may be withheld only where lessor has commercially reasonable objection to assignee or proposed use].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
